sections.title: What Makes Good Design?
sections.content:
# Design Principles for Maintainable Code

Good object-oriented design makes code:
- **Easy to understand**: Each class has one clear purpose
- **Easy to maintain**: Changes to one class don't break others
- **Easy to extend**: New features can be added without rewriting existing code
- **Easy to test**: Classes can be tested independently

Two key principles underlie all good design:
- **High cohesion**: Each class does one thing well
- **Low coupling**: Classes don't depend on each other's internals

The **SOLID principles** codify these ideas into actionable guidelines.

sections.title: High Cohesion, Low Coupling
SRP — Single Responsibility Principle
OCP — Open/Closed Principle
LSP — Liskov Substitution Principle
ISP — Interface Segregation Principle
DIP — Dependency Inversion Principle
Spotting Bad Design: God Classes
Refactoring: Breaking Apart Large Classes
What Does SRP Mean?
sections.content: Single Responsibility Principle means a class should have one reason to change — one responsibility. Each class should focus on one aspect of the system.

question.prompt: Which class violates the Single Responsibility Principle?
choices: A Student class that stores name, ID, and course list
A ReportGenerator that formats and outputs reports
A class that validates email, saves to database, and sends notifications
question.explanation: A class that validates, saves, and sends is doing three things. It has three reasons to change. Split into EmailValidator, UserRepository, and NotificationService.

sections.title: High Coupling Example
sections.content: High coupling occurs when classes depend on each other's internal details. Low coupling is when classes depend on abstractions or public interfaces.

choices: class A directly accesses class B's private variables
class A calls a public method on class B's interface
class A creates and manages class B's internal state
class A hardcodes class B's implementation details
question.explanation: Calling a public method creates loose coupling. The caller doesn't know or care about internal details, only the interface.

questions.prompt: Explain the Open/Closed Principle (OCP) with a concrete example. Show bad design that violates OCP and good design that follows it.
keyPoints: Open for extension: new types added without modifying existing code
Closed for modification: existing code doesn't change
Use polymorphism to handle different types
Eliminates if/elif chains for new types

questions.prompt: What is a God Class and why is it bad? Give an example and explain how to fix it.
questions.modelAnswer: A God Class is a class that does too much — it knows and controls everything. Bad: A ManagementSystem with methods for hiring, payroll, inventory, sales, reporting, etc. Problems: (1) Hard to understand — 100+ lines per method, (2) Hard to test — depends on everything, (3) Hard to reuse — can't use payroll logic without the whole system, (4) Changes break multiple features. Fix: Break it into smaller, focused classes by responsibility: HRManager, PayrollProcessor, InventoryManager, SalesManager, ReportGenerator. Each has one job, one reason to change.
keyPoints: God Class does too many unrelated things
Hard to understand, test, maintain, reuse
Multiple reasons to change = tight coupling
Each new class has one focused job

questions.prompt: How does the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) improve testability? Explain with an example.
questions.modelAnswer: DIP says depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations. This improves testability because you can inject mock implementations for testing. Example: Bad (violates DIP): UserService depends on MySQLDatabase directly — to test, you must use a real database. Good (follows DIP): UserService depends on abstract Database interface — you inject a MockDatabase for testing. Testing code: `service = UserService(MockDatabase())`. The service works without touching a real database. DIP decouples classes, making them independently testable.
keyPoints: DIP: depend on abstractions, not concrete classes
Enables unit testing without real dependencies
